title: Deploy a real Node app on your server

series.requires_description: nginx must be installed and running on port 80

problem: Deploy a real Node.js app on your server and keep it alive. The app must survive crashes and server reboots — if it goes down it should come back up on its own. The app will run on port 3000. We will put nginx in front of it in the next scenario.

setup.description: Make sure Node.js is installed and note the app repo you will be deploying.
setup.note: If node is not found install it: sudo yum install -y nodejs. If you need a newer version use nvm — curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash. App you will deploy: https://github.com/developit/node-hello

hints: Clone the app repo from the link in setup, cd into it and run npm install to install dependencies
Running node app.js directly is not enough — if it crashes or the server reboots the app dies. You need a process manager — pm2 is the industry standard, install it with npm install -g pm2
pm2 start app.js starts the app, pm2 startup generates the command to make it survive reboots, pm2 save locks in the current process list
solution:
1. Install git if missing:
   sudo yum install -y git
   # Needed to clone the repo.
2. Clone the app:
   git clone https://github.com/developit/node-hello
   # Free hello world Node app to deploy.
3. Enter the directory:
   cd node-hello
   # Move into the project folder.
4. Install dependencies:
   npm install
   # Installs everything listed in package.json.
5. Test it runs:
   node index.js
   # Confirm it starts, then Ctrl+C to stop.
6. Install pm2:
   sudo npm install -g pm2
   # Process manager — keeps your app alive after crashes and reboots.
7. Start with pm2:
   pm2 start index.js --name node-hello
   # Starts the app and labels it for easy management.
8. Check it is running:
   pm2 status
   # Should show node-hello as online.
9. Test the app:
   curl http://localhost:3000
   # Should return Hello World.
10. Generate startup hook:
   pm2 startup
   # pm2 prints a command — copy and run it.
11. Save process list:
   pm2 save
   # Locks in current processes to survive a reboot.
12. Verify after reboot:
   sudo reboot
   # Optional — SSH back in and run pm2 status to confirm.
